questions.question: Explain the concept of nested try statements in Java. When would you prefer nested try over a single try-catch block?
keyPoints: Definition: try block inside another try block
Allows handling exceptions at multiple granularity levels
Use when different code sections need different exception recovery strategies
Use when you want to provide fallback handling if inner catch fails
Example: File operations where file access and data parsing need separate handling

questions.question: What is the order of execution for catch and finally blocks in nested try statements? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Inner try-catch-finally executes before outer try-catch-finally
finally blocks always execute (unless System.exit() called)
JVM searches catch blocks from innermost to outermost
First matching catch handles the exception
If inner catch doesn't match, exception propagates to outer catch

questions.question: What is exception chaining in nested try statements? How can you preserve the original exception when throwing a new one?
keyPoints: Exception chaining preserves original exception context
Use initCause() method: throw new Exception("msg").initCause(original)
Or use constructor: new Exception("msg", original)
Without chaining, original exception details may be lost
Useful for debugging in nested exception scenarios

questions.question: Write a Java program demonstrating nested try statements for handling multiple types of exceptions in file processing.
keyPoints: Outer try: handles FileNotFoundException, IOException
Inner try: handles NumberFormatException, parsing errors
Different catch blocks for different exception types
Shows granular error handling and recovery

questions.question: Explain what happens if an exception occurs in a finally block of a nested try statement. How can this be prevented?
keyPoints: Exception in finally masks/overwrites the original exception
Original exception details are lost
Solution: Use try-finally inside catch or use exception chaining
Can also nest the finally handling in another try-catch
Important for proper debugging and error reporting
Inner try throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
Inner catch handles it, prints message, throws RuntimeException
Inner finally ALWAYS runs after catch, prints message
RuntimeException propagates to outer catch
Outer catch executes, prints message
Outer finally executes, prints message
Final output: Inner: Array index issue, Inner finally executed, Outer: Runtime error occurred, Outer finally executed

questions.question: Design a nested try-catch-finally structure for a banking transaction system that handles: (a) database connection failures, (b) invalid transaction amount, (c) insufficient funds. Explain your design with appropriate exception handling at each level.
keyPoints: Outer try: database operations - catches SQLException
Inner try: transaction validation - catches IllegalArgumentException for negative amounts
Inner catch: handles validation errors, allows retry or rollback
Finally: ensures connection closure regardless of success/failure
Shows layered exception handling appropriate for real-world scenarios
Use custom exceptions for better error identification
Implement proper rollback mechanism in finally or catch blocks

questions.question: Write a Java program that demonstrates proper exception handling in nested try blocks, including: (i) multiple levels of nesting, (ii) multiple catch blocks at each level, (iii) finally blocks at each level. Trace the execution for different types of exceptions thrown at different levels.
keyPoints: Minimum 3 levels of nesting
Each level should have at least 2 catch blocks
Each level should have finally blocks
Test with: Level1 exception, Level2 exception, Level3 exception
Show how exception propagates or gets handled at each level
Demonstrate catch block order importance
Illustrate finally execution order (inner to outer)
